items.question: By May, I ___ here for ten years.
explanations: 'Will work' is a single future action, not the duration up to May.
Correct! Duration up to a future point takes the future perfect continuous: 'will have been working'.
'Work' is present simple, not this future duration.
'Worked' is past simple, but May is in the future.

items.question: By then, she ___ studying for hours.
explanations: 'Will have studying' is missing 'been'. Use 'will have been studying'.
Correct! Future perfect continuous = will have been + -ing: 'will have been studying'.
'Will be studying' is the future continuous (in progress then), not duration up to then.
'Has been' is present perfect continuous, but this is about the future.

items.question: By 6, I ___ for eight hours.
explanations: 'Will have driven' focuses on completion, but 'for eight hours' stresses duration.
Correct! Duration up to a future point takes the future perfect continuous: 'will have been driving'.
'Drive' is present simple, not this future duration.
'Drove' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: By June, I ___ her for a year.
explanations: 'Know' is a stative verb and isn't used in the continuous.
Correct! State verbs take the future perfect simple: 'will have known'.
'Know' is present simple, but June is in the future.
'Knew' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: They ___ waiting long by the time we get there.
explanations: Correct! Negative future perfect continuous = won't have been + -ing.
'Won't waiting' is missing 'have been'.
'Don't have' is present simple, not this future form.
'Aren't waiting' is present continuous, but this is about the future.

items.question: He'll be tired — he ___ all night.
explanations: Correct! Explaining a future state through a long prior activity takes the future perfect continuous: 'will have been driving'.
'Will drive' is a single action, not the long activity that makes him tired.
'Drives' is present simple (a habit), not this future explanation.
'Drove' is past simple, but this is about the future.

items.question: How long ___ you ___ by 5pm?
explanations: Correct! Asking about duration up to a future point uses 'will you have been + -ing'.
'Do you travel' asks about a habit, not duration by 5pm.
'Will you travelling' is missing 'have been'.
'Are you travel' mixes structures. Use 'will you have been travelling'.

items.question: By Friday, I ___ the whole course.
explanations: 'Complete' describes a finished result, so the continuous doesn't fit.
Correct! A completed result takes the future perfect simple: 'will have completed'.
'Complete' is present simple, not this completed-by-Friday meaning.
'Completed' is past simple, but Friday is in the future.

items.question: Rewrite with the same meaning: 'I started this job in 2016. In 2026 I'll still be here.'
options: In 2026, I'll have been working here for ten years.
In 2026, I'll work here for ten years.
I worked here for ten years.
In 2026 I'll have had ten jobs.
explanations: Correct! Continuing from 2016 to 2026 = the future perfect continuous: 'will have been working for ten years'.
'Will work for ten years' doesn't capture the duration up to 2026.
'I worked' is past simple, but this looks forward to 2026.
This changes the meaning to ten different jobs.

items.question: By the end of today, we ___ for six hours.
explanations: Correct! Duration up to a future point takes the future perfect continuous: 'will have been rehearsing'.
'Will rehearse' is a single action, not the duration.
'Rehearse' is present simple, not this future duration.
'Rehearsed' is past simple, but this is about later today.
